era_context: Charlemagne's Frankish Empire at its height of expansion; Tang Dynasty under Dezong (r. 779–805, but Daizong still reigning in 778); Abbasid Caliphate under al-Hadi (r. 775–785); Byzantine Empire under Constantine VI with Irene as regent; Scandinavian Viking Age beginning.

events.title: Charlemagne's Roncevaux Pass campaign and the Song of Roland
events.description: Charlemagne led a military campaign into Iberia (modern Spain) in 778, responding to an appeal from the Umayyad emir of Zaragoza for aid against a rival. Charlemagne besieged Zaragoza but withdrew after learning of Saxon revolts in the north. On his return journey through the Pyrenees, his rearguard was ambushed at Roncevaux Pass by Basque forces (not Saracens, as later legend claimed). The ambush resulted in the death of Roland, Charlemagne's prefect of the Breton March, and other nobles. This event became the basis for the later epic poem, the Song of Roland (Chanson de Roland), one of the most important works of medieval literature. Historically, the campaign was a military failure but had significant cultural and legendary consequences.
key_figures: Roland (Prefect of the Breton March, d. 778)

sources.name: Song of Roland (Chanson de Roland), 11th–12th century

events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals are near-contemporary and record the campaign and the ambush at Roncevaux Pass. The date 778 is secure. However, the legendary version (Saracen ambush, etc.) differs from the historical account (Basque ambush).

events.title: Charlemagne's diet at Paderborn and Saxon consolidation
events.description: Following his return from the Iberian campaign, Charlemagne held a diet (assembly) at Paderborn in 778 to consolidate his authority and address the Saxon revolts. The diet was a major political and religious event, where Charlemagne demonstrated his power and began the process of Christianizing the Saxon territories. The assembly brought together Frankish nobles, bishops, and representatives from conquered territories. This event marked a turning point in Charlemagne's consolidation of the Frankish realm and his emergence as the dominant power in Western Europe.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals record the diet at Paderborn in 778. The date is secure.

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Daizong's reign (ongoing)
events.description: Emperor Daizong (r. 762–779) was still reigning in 778, in the later years of his reign. Daizong's rule was marked by efforts to restore imperial authority after the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763) and to deal with the power of regional military governors (jiedushi). In 778, Daizong was aging and his health was declining; he would die in 779, succeeded by his son Dezong. The year 778 represents a period of relative stability in the Tang Dynasty, though the underlying tensions between central authority and regional military power remained unresolved.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Daizong's reign dates (762–779) are secure, but specific events within 778 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: al-Hadi's reign (ongoing)
events.description: Caliph al-Hadi (r. 775–785) was in the middle of his reign in 778. His rule was characterized by efforts to consolidate power after the civil war following al-Mansur's death and to deal with succession disputes. The Barmakid family of viziers was rising in influence during this period. In 778, the caliphate was administratively stable but politically tense, with various factions competing for influence and succession.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Al-Hadi's reign dates are secure, but specific events within 778 are not well-documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Roncevaux Pass ambush is often portrayed in legend as a Saracen (Muslim) attack, but modern scholarship, based on the Royal Frankish Annals and other contemporary sources, identifies the attackers as Basques, not Muslims. The Song of Roland, composed centuries later (11th–12th century), transformed the historical event into a religious and heroic narrative that bears little resemblance to the actual ambush. This is a significant case of legendary distortion of historical fact. The diet at Paderborn in 778 is confirmed, but its exact significance and the extent of Charlemagne's control over the Saxons at this point are debated by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 778 CE is moderate, primarily from Frankish sources. The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary accounts of Charlemagne's activities, making this one of the better-documented years in early medieval Western Europe. However, sources from other regions (China, Islamic world, Byzantine Empire) are less detailed for this specific year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, demographic, and technological changes are largely invisible. The Roncevaux Pass event is historically significant but has been heavily distorted by later legend, making it a cautionary example of how medieval sources can be unreliable. The year 778 is significant primarily for Frankish history; it is less important in the broader context of world history.

graph_edges.note: The Roncevaux Pass campaign led to the diet at Paderborn, where Charlemagne consolidated his authority
The Roncevaux Pass ambush became the basis for the Song of Roland, composed centuries later
Daizong's reign in 778 ended with his death in 779, succeeded by Dezong
